title: How AI cleaned up 15,000 recipes without breaking the site
description: A walkthrough of the CookJunkie recipe audit, including why the AI never touched production data directly.
bodyMd:
## The problem

CookJunkie has 15,000 recipes. They migrated over from the old WordPress site, but years of inconsistent data entry meant most of them had *something* off — instructions jammed into one long paragraph, difficulty levels spelled six different ways, prep and cook times missing, ingredients stuck in a freeform text blob, and nutrition facts that no longer matched the recipe. Across 15,000 rows, the majority of the catalog needed at least one fix.

Fixing that by hand wasn't realistic, and handing the whole pile to an AI in one shot wasn't either — there'd be no way to review, no way to roll back, and any bad output would land straight in a live cookbook.

The approach that did work was building a workflow around the AI rather than giving it unrestricted access.

## Step 1: Pull the data down

First, copy all 15,000 recipes out of the live database into plain files, one file per recipe. The AI never points at production; it works from a copy, and the worst thing it can do is write a wrong file.

## Step 2: Split it into manageable batches

15,000 recipes is a lot to throw at anything at once, so the copies get split into small batches of a handful of recipes each. The size is chosen so a single worker can pay attention to the details while still making the batch worth queuing up.

## Step 3: Let AI work one batch at a time

For each batch, I launch ten AI workers in parallel, each handed a single recipe and a specific checklist:

- **Instructions packed into one blob.** `"a. Preheat oven b. Mix flour…"` becomes a proper step-by-step list.
- **Inconsistent difficulty.** Five variants of *easy* collapse into one.
- **Missing times.** Recipes with no prep or cook time get reasonable values.
- **Messy ingredients.** Freeform text like *3 tbsp diced tomato* is split into quantity, unit, and base ingredient so the site can match it against what a user has in their pantry.
- **Stale nutrition.** If the ingredients have changed since the last calculation, the calories and macros are recalculated to match.
- **Missing tags.** Obvious cuisines or diets (Italian, vegetarian, and so on) are filled in.

The full spec each worker was handed, including the legacy fields to leave alone and the exact rules for every fix, is here: [audit_recipes.md](/audit_recipes.md).

Each worker writes its proposed fixes to its own file, containing just the fields it wants to change. Nothing lands in the database at this stage. The output is a directory of "here's what I'd change" files.

## Step 4: Review before anything goes live

Because every proposal is a plain file on disk, each batch is reviewable as a straightforward before/after. I can look at what the AI suggested for every recipe and accept, edit, or discard it.

A real example from the run: [before](/recipe-before.json) and [after](/recipe-after.json), the same recipe pre- and post-audit.

## Step 5: Apply, with a dry run

A separate script reads all the proposal files and pushes the changes into the database. It has a preview mode (*show me what would change, but don't write anything*) for one more sanity check before anything lands. If something goes wrong after the fact, the proposal files are still on disk for revert or re-run.

## Why the separation matters

If the AI had been writing straight to the database, a single bad run could have corrupted thousands of recipes with no easy way back. Splitting the work into *propose → review → apply* keeps every step recoverable and every change auditable. The AI does the part it's good at — proposing fixes quickly across a large catalog — without ever being given permission to commit those fixes on its own.

The substantive engineering was in the workflow, not the prompt.
